metadata.summary: Install and launch Puppeteer (headless Chrome via DevTools Protocol), configure browser flags, navigate pages, and wait for DOM/network conditions reliably.

content.title: Puppeteer: Launch, Navigation & Wait Strategies
content.summary: Puppeteer is Google's Node.js library for controlling Chrome/Chromium via the DevTools Protocol. This methodology covers installation, browser launch configuration, page navigation, and the correct wait strategies for SPA and classic sites.

why.p: Puppeteer gives a programmable Chrome surface with full DevTools access — request interception, coverage, tracing, CDP sessions. Understanding the right launch flags and wait strategies prevents the two most common failure modes: scripts that hang on networkidle0 for WebSocket-heavy SPAs and scripts that break in CI because of missing sandbox flags.

list.item: Headless Chrome scripting jobs that need DevTools Protocol features (CDP sessions, request interception, coverage, tracing).
Generating PDFs or screenshots from rendered HTML inside a Node-only pipeline.
Light scraping where stealth is already handled (reCAPTCHA-free targets) and you want minimum dependencies.
Running on serverless (chromium binary via @sparticuz/chromium) where Playwright bundles are too large.
Cross-browser testing — use Playwright (Firefox + WebKit are first-class there; Puppeteer is Chromium-only).
E2E test suites with parallelism, retries, fixtures — Playwright Test or Cypress give you that for free.
Long-running scraping farms against bot-defended targets — Playwright + stealth or a managed service.

section.name: installation
section.p: Install puppeteer (bundles Chromium) or puppeteer-core (bring your own browser binary):

example.title: Install
example.code: npm install puppeteer # or lighter version without bundled browser npm install puppeteer-core

rule.statement: Set PUPPETEER_CACHE_DIR to a writable path in CI; the default ~/.cache/puppeteer breaks in read-only containers. Pin the chromium revision in the lockfile so headless behaviour is reproducible across agent runs.
rule.rationale: Chrome auto-update via puppeteer install drifts the bundled version; pinning prevents silent CI failures.

section.name: launch-config
section.p: Standard launch configuration for containerised / CI environments:

example.title: Standard launch
example.note: --no-sandbox and --disable-setuid-sandbox are required in Docker/CI where the process runs as root.

example.title: Custom user agent and viewport

rule.statement: Always call browser.close() in a finally block. Orphan Chromium processes pin GBs of RAM; a single browser instance under page churn must be recycled every N pages.
rule.rationale: Node process exit does not guarantee browser process cleanup; finally ensures cleanup on both normal exit and unhandled exceptions.

section.name: navigation
section.p: Navigate to pages and handle post-navigation flows:

example.title: Navigate with options

rule.statement: Prefer domcontentloaded + explicit waitForSelector over networkidle0 for SPA targets. The networkidle0 condition hangs forever on sites with long-poll or WebSocket traffic; agents loop until timeout.
rule.rationale: networkidle0 waits for zero in-flight network requests for 500ms — SPAs with analytics pings, WebSockets, or SSE never reach that state.

section.name: wait-strategies
section.p: All reliable wait strategies available in Puppeteer:

example.title: Wait strategies

antipattern.description: Using await page.waitForTimeout(N) to pause execution.
antipattern.reason: waitForTimeout was deprecated and removed in Puppeteer 22. Use new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, N)) or preferably a targeted waitForSelector/waitForFunction instead of fixed sleeps.
